bibliographic_data.title: On the Formation of Galaxy Halos: Comparing NGC 5128 and the Local Group Members
bibliographic_data.abstract: The metallicity distribution function (MDF) for the old red-giant stars in the halo of NGC 5128, the nearest giant elliptical galaxy, is virtually identical with the MDF for the old-disk stars in the LMC and also strongly resembles the halo MDF in M31. These galaxies all have high mean halo metallicities (⟨m/H⟩≃−0.4) with very small proportions of low-metallicity stars. These observations reinforce the view that metal-rich halos are quite normal for large galaxies of all types. Such systems are unlikely to have built up by accretion of pre-existing, gas-free small satellite galaxies, unless these satellites had an extremely shallow mass distribution (∆log N/∆log M ≳−1). We suggest that the halo of NGC 5128 is more likely to have assembled from hierarchical merging of gas-rich lumps in which the bulk of star formation took place during or after the merger stage.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study compares the metallicity distribution function (MDF) of NGC 5128's halo with that of the LMC and M31, suggesting a similar formation mechanism involving hierarchical merging of gas-rich lumps.
core_content.key_methodology: Using deep (V, I) photometry from HST/WFPC2 to obtain color-magnitude diagrams for fields in the halo of NGC 5128, revealing the metallicity distribution function (MDF) for old-halo red-giant branch stars.
outcomes: The MDFs of NGC 5128's halo and the LMC are virtually identical with a high mean metallicity peak at [m/H] ≃−0.4, while the Milky Way's halo has a much lower mean metallicity.
The observed MDFs suggest that large galaxies like NGC 5128 likely formed through hierarchical merging of gas-rich lumps rather than accretion of pre-existing, gas-free small satellite galaxies.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This study investigates the formation mechanisms of galaxy halos by comparing the metallicity distribution function (MDF) for old red-giant stars in the halo of NGC 5128 with that of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and M31. The results indicate a high mean metallicity peak at [m/H] ≃−0.4, similar to both NGC 5128 and the LMC, but in contrast to the Milky Way's halo which has a much lower mean metallicity. These findings support the hypothesis that large galaxies like NGC 5128 formed through hierarchical merging of gas-rich lumps rather than accretion of pre-existing, gas-free small satellite galaxies.
